utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: it's good to see you as well . so i know that the nurse told you about dax . i'd like to tell dax a little bit about you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay ? so , andrew is a 62-year-old male with a past medical history significant for a kidney transplant , hypothyroidism , and arthritis , who presents today with complaints of joint pain . andrew , what's going on with your joint ? what happened ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , so , over the the weekend , we've been moving boxes up and down our basements stairs , and by the end of the day my knees were just killing me .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . is , is one knee worse than the other ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and did you , did you injure one of them ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , uh , i've had some knee problems in the past but i think it was just the repetition and the weight of the boxes .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . and , and what have you taken for the pain ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: a little tylenol . i iced them for a bit . nothing really seemed to help , though .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . um , and does it prevent you from doing , like , your activities of daily living , like walking and exercising and things like that ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , saturday night it actually kept me up for a bit . they were pretty sore .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm . okay . and any other symptoms like fever or chills ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: joint pain ... i mean , like muscle aches ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . now , i know that you've had the kidney transplant a few years ago for some polycystic kidneys .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: um , how are you doing with that ? i know that you told dr. gutierrez-

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right . and you're taking your immunosuppressive medications ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . um , and did they have anything to say ? i have n't gotten any reports from them , so ...

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , n- nothing out of the ordinary , from what they reported .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . um , and in terms of your hyperthyroidism , how are you doing with the synthroid ? are you doing okay ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yes . okay . and any fatigue ? weight gain ? anything like that that you've noticed ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , nothing out of the ordinary .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and just in general , you know , i know that we've kind of battled with your arthritis .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you know , it's hard because you ca n't take certain medications 'cause of your kidney transplant .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so other than your knees , any other joint pain or anything like that ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: every once in a while , my elbow , but nothing , nothing out of the ordinary .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . now i know the nurse did a review of systems sheet when you checked in . any other symptoms i might have missed ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: anything like that w- ... okay . all right . well , i wan na go ahead and do a quick physical exam , all right ? hey , dragon , show me the vital signs . so here in the office , your vital signs look good . you do n't have a fever , which is good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: your heart rate and your , uh , blood pressure look fine . i'm just gon na check some things out , and i'll let you know what i find , okay ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , so on physical examination , on your heart exam , i do appreciate a little two out of six systolic ejection murmur-

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: . which we've heard in the past . okay , so that seems stable . on your knee exam , there is some edema and some erythema of your right knee , but your left knee looks fine , okay ? um , you do have some pain to palpation of the right knee and some decreased range of motion , um , on exam , okay ? so what does that mean ? so we'll go ahead and we'll see if we can take a look at some of these things . i know that they did an x-ray before you came in , okay ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so let's take a look at that .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey , dragon , show me the right knee x-ray . so here's the r- here's your right knee x-ray . this basically shows that there's good bony alignment . there's no acute fracture , which is not surprising , based on the history .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay ? hey , dragon , show me the labs . and here , looking at your lab results , you know , your white blood cell count is not elevated , which is good . you know , we get concerned about that in somebody who's immunocompromised .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and it looks like your kidney function is also very good . so i'm , i'm very happy about that .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay ? so i just wan na go over a little bit about my assessment and my plan for you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so for your knee pain , i think that this is an acute exacerbation of your arthritis , okay ? so i wan na go ahead and if ... and prescribe some ultram 50 milligrams every six hours as needed .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay ? i also wan na go ahead and just order an autoimmune panel , okay ? hey , dragon , order an autoimmune panel . and you know , i , i want , i want you to just take it easy for right now , and if your symptoms continue , we'll talk about further imaging and possibly referral to physical therapy , okay ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: for your second problem , your hypothyroidism , i wan na go ahead and continue you on this ... on the synthroid , and i wan na go ahead and order some thyroid labs , okay ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey , dragon , order a thyroid panel . and then for your last problem , the arthritis , you know , we just kinda talked about that . you know , it's gon na be a struggle for you because again , you ca n't take some of those anti-inflammatory medications because of your kidney transplant , so ...

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you know , let's see how we do over the next couple weeks , and again , we'll refer you to physical therapy if we need to , okay ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . hey , dragon , finalize the note .

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 62-year-old male with a history of kidney transplant, hypothyroidism, and arthritis.
Joint pain.
The patient reports bilateral knee pain after moving boxes over the weekend.
The doctor asks if one knee is worse than the other.
The patient reports the pain is equal in both knees.
The doctor asks if the patient injured one of his knees.
The patient reports past knee problems and attributes current pain to the weight and repetition of moving boxes.
The doctor asks what the patient has taken for the pain.
The patient has taken Tylenol and used ice, but reports no relief.
The doctor asks if the pain prevents the patient from performing activities of daily living.
The patient reports the knee soreness interrupted his sleep on Saturday night.
The doctor asks if the patient has fever or chills.
The patient denies fever or chills.
The doctor asks if the patient has muscle aches.
The patient denies muscle aches.
The doctor asks if the patient has nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.
The patient denies nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.
Kidney transplant
The doctor asks if the patient's health status regarding his kidney transplant is okay.
The patient reports he is doing well.
The doctor asks if the patient is taking his immunosuppressant medications.
The patient confirms he is taking his immunosuppressant medications.
Hypothyroidism
The doctor asks how the patient is doing with Synthroid.
The patient reports he is doing well.
The doctor asks if the patient is taking Synthroid regularly.
The patient confirms he takes Synthroid regularly.
The doctor asks if the patient has noticed fatigue or weight gain.
The patient denies fatigue or weight gain.
The doctor asks if the patient has any other joint pain besides his knees.
The patient reports occasional elbow pain.
The doctor asks if the patient has headaches.
The patient denies headaches.
The doctor declares the patient's vital signs are normal and he is afebrile.
The doctor asks if the palpation causes pain.
The patient reports tenderness.
The doctor identifies a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur on cardiac exam.
The doctor notes edema, erythema, tenderness, and decreased range of motion in the right knee, with a normal left knee exam.
The right knee x-ray shows good bony alignment and no acute fractures.
The patient's white blood cell count is within normal limits.
The patient's kidney function is normal.
The doctor prescribes Ultram 50 mg every 6 hours as needed.
The doctor orders an autoimmune panel and advises the patient to rest his knees.
The doctor orders a thyroid panel and continues the patient on Synthroid.
The doctor will consider a referral to physical therapy if symptoms persist.
The doctor asks if the patient has any questions.
The patient has no questions.
